<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) with zero external coupling confirms the Notion client is cleanly isolated — changes here carry no ripple risk to other zones.
- The presence of a store-contract test suggests an interface-driven adapter pattern where all store backends are validated against a shared contract, enabling safe substitution of future adapters.
- This zone is likely a detached fragment of the broader 'Remote Sync Adapters' zone (algorithmicId: store); consider whether the Notion client source file belongs there to consolidate the store layer.
- notion-client.ts is the only production source file in this zone, co-located with three unit tests; algorithmically it separated from the main store zone, but architecturally it may be a candidate for consolidation into Remote Sync Adapters.
- Zero coupling is ideal for a leaf-node adapter — no upstream zone depends on this Notion-specific implementation, keeping vendor lock-in contained.
- The notion-store-client zone has no corresponding e2e test coverage visible in the e2e-tests zone — the Notion sync path may be untested at the CLI boundary level.
- notion-store-client and e2e-tests are both zero-coupling zones but have no structural relationship to each other — the Notion adapter is covered only by unit tests, not e2e tests, creating a coverage gap at the CLI boundary.
- The zone contains exactly one production source file (notion-client.ts) surrounded by three test files — algorithmically isolated because tests inflate the intra-zone edge weight artificially. The Louvain community boundary here reflects test proximity, not architectural intent.
- notion-client.ts is the sole production source in this zone; its separation from the `store` zone is a Louvain artifact caused by co-located tests inflating intra-community edge weights. Merging notion-client.ts into the store zone would consolidate all adapter implementations under one boundary and eliminate the spurious zone boundary.
- The zone ID embeds a vendor name ('notion') rather than an abstract adapter role — if a second sync adapter is added (e.g. linear, github), following this naming convention would spawn a new vendor-named zone per adapter rather than consolidating under a generic adapter zone, causing zone proliferation.
- store-contract.test.ts validates an interface contract whose definition must reside in another zone (likely store/remote-sync-adapters) — the test file creates a hidden semantic coupling that the import graph cannot detect, meaning the contract can drift silently if the source interface changes without triggering a re-import edge.
- The 3:1 test-to-source ratio (3 test files, 1 production file) is the highest ratio in any non-trivial zone — this may indicate the adapter boundary is over-tested at the unit level while being under-tested at the integration level, misallocating test effort.
- Rename the zone ID from 'notion-store-client' to a role-based name (e.g. 'notion-adapter') and establish a convention that all sync adapter zones follow '<vendor>-adapter' rather than '<vendor>-store-client' to prevent naming drift as new adapters are added.
- store-contract.test.ts creates a semantic coupling to the adapter interface defined in the store zone that is invisible to the import graph. Document this cross-zone contract dependency explicitly (e.g. a comment header in store-contract.test.ts naming the source interface file) so refactors of the store interface automatically prompt review of this test.
